title: Sanity Product Terminology

entries.definition: The Content Operating System for structured content, real-time collaboration, and composable APIs
entries.context: Sanity stores structured content that your front end queries via API.

entries.definition: Sanity's real-time backend data store for structured content
entries.context: Store and query all your content in the Content Lake.

entries.definition: Sanity's open-source query language (Graph-Relational Object Queries)
entries.context: Use GROQ to filter and project your content exactly how your frontend needs it.

entries.definition: Sanity's open-source content editing environment (Sanity Studio)
entries.context: Configure your Studio with custom document views and plugins.

entries.definition: Sanity's structured rich text format, stored as JSON and renderable anywhere
entries.context: Use Portable Text for rich text that works across web, mobile, and print.

entries.definition: Feature for viewing content in different states (draft, published, release)
entries.context: Switch between Perspectives to preview how content looks before publishing.

entries.definition: Feature for staging and coordinating content updates as a group
entries.context: Group related changes into a Release, then publish everything at once.

entries.definition: Sanity API for invoking AI-powered actions (translate, generate, transform) on content documents
entries.context: Use Agent Actions to translate documents into any target locale.

entries.definition: The platform category Sanity defines for structured content management in the AI era
entries.context: Sanity is the Content Operating System for teams building modern digital experiences.

entries.definition: Sanity Studio plugin for visual editing with live preview of front-end applications
entries.context: Connect your Next.js app to the Presentation Tool for click-to-edit overlays.

entries.definition: A named collection of documents within a Sanity project. Each dataset is an isolated content store.
entries.context: Create a staging dataset to test content changes before promoting to production.

entries.definition: A button in the Studio publish menu that performs an operation on a document (publish, unpublish, duplicate, delete)
entries.context: Add a custom document action to trigger translation when content is published.

entries.definition: A named, typed input within a Sanity document schema. Fields define the structure of your content.
entries.context: Add a slug field to generate URL-friendly identifiers from the title.

entries.definition: Do not describe Sanity as a CMS. Use "Content Operating System" or "structured content platform" instead.
Avoid this category label for Sanity. Use "Content Operating System" or describe specific capabilities instead.

title: German (Germany) Style Guide

children.text: Target audience: Developers building with Sanity, content editors managing structured content, and technical leaders evaluating content infrastructure in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland.
Gender guidance: Use gender-inclusive language. Prefer the gender colon form (“Entwickler:innen”, “Redakteur:innen”) for inclusive references to people.
Address the reader with “Sie” (formal). Avoid unnecessary anglicisms when established German terms exist, but keep Sanity product names in English.
Do:
“Konfigurieren Sie Ihr Studio” (formal address)
“Inhalte im Content Lake speichern” (keep product name in English)
“Datensatz” for dataset, “Feld” for field
Do not:
“Konfiguriere dein Studio” (too informal)
“Inhalte im Inhaltssee speichern” (do not translate product names)

title: French (France) Style Guide

children.text: Target audience: Developers integrating Sanity into front-end applications, and content teams managing editorial workflows in France.

Follow Académie française recommendations for French equivalents of English tech terms where established alternatives exist. Keep Sanity product names (Studio, Content Lake, GROQ, Portable Text) in English.
Use “vous” (formal). Write in active voice.
Do:
“Interrogez vos contenus avec GROQ” (keep product name, use vous)
“le jeu de données de production” (use French term for dataset)
“action de document” for document action, “champ” for field
Do not:
“Querier vos contenus” (franglais verb)
“le Content Lake de Sanity” without first introducing the term in context

title: Japanese (Japan) Style Guide

children.text: Target audience: Developers evaluating Sanity for content infrastructure, and content editors who will use Studio daily in Japan.

Use desu/masu (です/ます) form throughout. Keep Sanity product names in their original Latin script. Write technical terms in katakana when no established Japanese equivalent exists. Use full-width punctuation (。、) consistently.
Do:
“Sanity Studioを設定します” (product name in Latin script, desu/masu form)
“コンテンツをContent Lakeに保存します” (product name in Latin script)
“データセット” for dataset, “フィールド” for field
Do not:
“サニティ” (do not transliterate “Sanity” to katakana)
“コンテントレイク” (do not transliterate “Content Lake”)

title: How to Run a Simultaneous Product Launch Across 12 Markets
excerpt: A practical, battle-tested guide to coordinating a global product launch where all markets go live within the same 24-hour window—covering timezones, localization, regulatory review, and the critical role of structured content.

children.text: Why a true simultaneous launch is harder than it looks

Coordinating a product launch across 12 markets in a single 24-hour window is less about heroics and more about ruthless systems thinking. The complexity doesn’t scale linearly: going from 3 to 12 markets feels less like adding 9 and more like multiplying by 4. Every timezone, language, legal regime, and channel adds another axis of failure. If you don’t design for this from day one, you’ll end up with half the world launching late, another quarter blocked by regulators, and the rest improvising with outdated assets.

The teams that pull this off consistently don’t rely on last-minute coordination. They build a launch machine: a shared calendar that runs on UTC, a structured content model that separates meaning from presentation, a localization pipeline that can be parallelized, and a clear decision tree for what happens when (not if) something slips in one region. Think less "big bang" and more "orchestrated relay" where every handoff is pre-rehearsed.
Start with the clock: designing a timezone-safe launch plan

The first mistake I see is planning around local time instead of a single global reference. For a 12-market launch, everything lives in UTC. Your master launch plan, your embargo times, your content scheduling, your incident war room—all in UTC. Local teams can translate to their own timezones, but the source of truth never changes.

Pick a 24-hour window where your most operationally critical regions overlap. For example, if your core teams sit in San Francisco, London, and Singapore, a common pattern is a UTC-based window that starts when APAC is beginning its day and ends when the US West Coast is still online. Then define three waves inside that window: APAC go-live, EMEA go-live, Americas go-live. Each wave has a clear T- timeline (T-72h, T-24h, T-4h, T-1h, T+1h) with specific checks: content freeze, legal sign-off, deployment readiness, and channel scheduling.
Operationally, this means:
A single, shared runbook in UTC with per-market annotations
A staffed virtual war room that hands off between regions (APAC → EMEA → Americas)
Pre-scheduled content in tools that support exact UTC timestamps (not "local time" guesses)
If you don’t normalize to UTC, you will eventually watch a market go live 12 hours early because someone misread "midnight".
Structured content: the backbone of a global launch
The only way to keep 12 markets aligned is to stop treating content as a collection of files and start treating it as structured data. That means:
Product name, tagline, and value props as discrete fields
Feature descriptions as reusable, versioned components
Legal disclaimers as attachable entities by market
Screenshots, videos, and UI strings referenced by IDs, not hard-coded into copy
For example, instead of a single "Launch Blog Post – EN.docx" that gets emailed around, you define a content type
launch_announcement
with fields like
headline
subheadline
core_benefit
feature_list
cta_label
, and
market_overrides
. Translators and local marketers work inside that structure. When the product name changes for one market, you update the
product_name
field for that locale and every asset that references it (web, email, in-app, social) updates automatically.

This is what makes a simultaneous launch possible: you can freeze the schema (what content you need and where it appears) while still iterating on the actual text and assets per market. Without this, you’ll be manually editing 40+ versions of the same sentence across 10 tools at 3 a.m.
Building a translation and localization pipeline that can survive reality
A 12-market launch is not just about translation; it’s about localization under time pressure. You need a pipeline that assumes late changes, partial readiness, and market-specific constraints.
A practical pattern looks like this:
Source content freeze (T-14 to T-10 days)
: Freeze English source content at the field level in your CMS. No more structural changes after this point.
Batch export to TMS (Translation Management System)
: Push structured fields (not PDFs) into your TMS with clear context: screenshots, character limits, tone guidelines, and notes about what can or cannot be changed.
Parallel translation + in-market review (T-10 to T-5 days)
: Linguists translate; in-market marketers review for nuance, cultural fit, and brand voice.
LQA (Linguistic QA) in product and channels (T-5 to T-2 days)
: Review translations in context—inside the app, on landing pages, in emails. This is where you catch truncation, broken layouts, and awkward phrasing.
Lock critical strings (T-2 days)
: Freeze UI strings and high-risk copy (e.g., legal claims). Non-critical marketing copy can still flex closer to launch.
The key is to separate
critical path content
(UI strings, legal disclaimers, pricing) from
flexible content
(social copy variants, blog intros). Critical path content must complete the full pipeline; flexible content can be handled with lighter-weight workflows if needed.
When your product name doesn’t travel: handling naming and connotation issues

If you haven’t had a product name backfire in another language yet, you either haven’t launched globally or you’ve been very lucky. I’ve seen an English name that sounded modern and sharp turn out to be slang for "useless" in Brazilian Portuguese, and another that was uncomfortably close to a vulgar term in German.
You can’t bolt this check on at the end. For any global launch, run
name and tagline vetting
early:
Shortlist names in English
Run them through native speakers in your top 10–15 languages
Check for negative meanings, awkward phonetics, and unintended associations

Suppose your product is called "Flux" in English. In one market, local teams flag that it sounds like a word associated with illness. Instead of forcing a bad fit, your structured content model should allow a
localized product name
field per market. Maybe Japan uses "Fluxo", Germany uses "FluxPro", and the rest of the world uses "Flux". Your system should:
Store
product_name_global
and
product_name_local
per locale
Use
product_name_local
in all customer-facing copy for that market
Keep
product_name_global
for internal systems, analytics, and cross-market reporting
This way, you avoid the all-or-nothing decision of "rename globally or live with it" and still maintain operational coherence.
Regulatory review: designing for asynchronous approvals

Regulatory review is where simultaneous launches go to die if you don’t plan for it. Different jurisdictions care about different things: data privacy in the EU, financial claims in the US, promotional wording in certain APAC markets. The mistake is treating legal as a single global gatekeeper.
Instead, map
regulatory criticality by market and asset type
Which markets require pre-approval for marketing claims?
Which assets must be reviewed (e.g., landing pages, emails, in-app flows, T&Cs)?
What are the typical SLAs for each legal or compliance team?
Then build a
regulatory matrix
into your launch plan. For example:
EU markets: require DPO and legal review for any new data processing or profiling language
US: require legal review for any performance claims ("2x faster", "50% cheaper")
Japan: require local legal partner review for promotional campaigns with prizes
Operationally, this means:
Tagging content fields that are "reg-sensitive" in your CMS
Routing only those fields/variants to the relevant legal queues
Tracking approvals per market and per asset, not as a single global "approved" flag
When (not if) one market’s regulator is late, your structured content lets you
de-scope
that market’s launch (e.g., use a more conservative claim set) without blocking the other 11.
Asset localization: screenshots, videos, and UI strings

Copy is only half the story. Visual assets are where many teams underestimate the work. A truly global launch means your screenshots, videos, and UI strings reflect the actual product experience in each market.
For screenshots:
Maintain a
screenshot matrix
: platforms (iOS, Android, web) × languages × key flows
Use automation where possible (e.g., scripted screenshot capture) but always budget time for manual curation
Localize visible text, currency, date formats, and even example names where culturally relevant
For videos:
Decide upfront which markets get
subtitles only
vs.
voiceover
vs.
fully localized cuts
Keep your master edit in a way that supports easy text layer swaps (e.g., separate text layers in After Effects, not baked into footage)
Store video text (on-screen copy, captions) as structured fields so they can go through the same translation pipeline
For UI strings:
Treat your product as a first-class localization client, not an afterthought
Use a single source of truth for strings with locale variants, versioning, and rollback
Run
in-context LQA
: have native speakers click through the localized build and log issues before launch
The goal is that when your Japanese customer clicks from a Japanese ad to a Japanese landing page and into a Japanese UI, nothing breaks immersion.
Orchestrating channel go-live: web, app, email, and paid

A simultaneous launch is only as synchronized as your channels. I’ve seen launches where the app store listing updated 6 hours after the press release, and paid ads started driving traffic to a 404 because the landing page publish failed in one region.
To avoid this, define a
channel choreography
per market:
T-24h
: All web content staged and QA’d in non-indexed environments
T-12h
: App builds approved and in phased release (if applicable), store listings scheduled
T-4h
: Email campaigns loaded, QA’d, and scheduled to send at local time X (mapped to UTC)
T-1h
: Paid campaigns set to "paused" with start times aligned to launch window
: Flip web and in-app feature flags, unpause paid, release press/PR

Use feature flags aggressively. For example, deploy the new UI globally behind a flag days before launch. At T-0, you’re not deploying; you’re toggling. This dramatically reduces risk and gives you a clean rollback path per market if something breaks.
The handoff game: running a 24-hour virtual war room
You cannot run a 12-market launch from a single timezone without burning people out and increasing risk. Instead, treat it as a relay race with a staffed virtual war room that follows the sun.
Structure it like this:
APAC shift
: Owns final checks and go-live for APAC markets, preps EMEA handoff notes
EMEA shift
: Monitors APAC post-launch, executes EMEA go-live, preps Americas handoff
Americas shift
: Monitors EMEA, executes Americas go-live, runs global post-mortem prep
Each shift lead owns a
single source of truth dashboard
Launch status by market (Not started / In progress / Live / Blocked)
Channel status (Web, App, Email, Paid, PR) per market
Known issues and workarounds

Handoffs are written, not verbal. At the end of each shift, the lead updates the runbook with what changed, what’s at risk, and what decisions are pending. That way, when EMEA logs on, they’re not digging through Slack history to figure out why Brazil’s paid campaigns are still paused.
Designing for failure: fallbacks, rollbacks, and partial launches

The difference between an amateur and a seasoned global launch is not whether things go wrong; it’s how gracefully they fail. You should assume that at least one market will have a blocker: a late legal approval, a payment integration issue, or a last-minute bug.
Design your plan so that
one market’s problem doesn’t become everyone’s problem
Use
per-market feature flags
so you can hold back functionality in one region without touching others
Prepare
tiered messaging
: a full-feature launch narrative and a "limited availability" variant that removes risky claims
Define
go/no-go criteria per market
, not just globally (e.g., "If local legal hasn’t approved by T-4h, we launch with conservative copy set B")
After launch, run a structured retrospective within 72 hours. Capture:
Which content fields caused the most friction
Where translation or legal bottlenecks occurred
Which handoffs were noisy or unclear
Then update your structured content model and runbook accordingly. A global launch machine is never "done"; it’s iterated into reliability.
Bringing it all together

A simultaneous 12-market launch is not about heroics on launch day; it’s about the unglamorous work you do weeks earlier: defining a structured content model, mapping regulatory requirements, building a localization pipeline that respects reality, and designing your systems around UTC and feature flags.

When you get this right, launch day feels almost boring. Markets go live in sequence, content is consistent but culturally tuned, regulators aren’t surprised, and your teams in Tokyo, Berlin, and New York are all telling the same story in their own words. That’s the real goal: not just "everyone launched on the same day," but "everyone launched the same product, with the right promise, to the right audience, at the right moment."

title: Why Translation Glossaries Are Your Most Underrated Content Asset
excerpt: Most teams treat translation glossaries as dusty spreadsheets. In reality, they’re high-leverage infrastructure—if you treat them as structured metadata that both humans and AI can actually use in real time.

children.text: Most teams think of translation glossaries as a compliance checkbox: a spreadsheet someone in marketing or localization maintains, occasionally emailed to vendors and promptly forgotten. That mindset leaves a huge amount of value on the table. When you treat your glossary as structured metadata—machine-readable, queryable, enforced at runtime—it stops being documentation and starts being infrastructure. And like any good piece of infrastructure, it quietly prevents expensive mistakes long before they reach your customers.
The core shift is simple: move from a static list of terms to a structured model of how those terms behave in language. A modern glossary entry shouldn’t just be
term
and
translation
. It should capture fields like:
term
: the source string (e.g.,
Acme Cloud Hub
status
approved
do-not-translate
deprecated
experimental
part of speech
: noun, verb, adjective, etc.
grammatical gender
(for target languages that need it)
usage context
: product area, audience, register, examples
Once this information is encoded as metadata instead of buried in comments or separate tabs, both human translators and AI agents can make correct decisions automatically, at scale.
Take the simplest but most common case:
product names that must never be translated
. In a spreadsheet, you might have a note in a comments column: “Don’t translate
Acme Cloud Hub
.” That’s fragile. People miss comments. AI models never see them. In a metadata-driven glossary, you’d have an entry like:
term:
Acme Cloud Hub
status:
do-not-translate
part of speech:
proper noun
usage context:
core product name; keep English in all locales; allow declension in Slavic languages but not translation
Now your translation pipeline can enforce this. A human translator working in a CAT tool sees a clear, machine-enforced rule. An AI translation agent can be prompted with: “For any term with status
do-not-translate
, copy the source term exactly, except for allowed inflection rules.” You’ve turned tribal knowledge into a guardrail.
The value becomes even clearer with
technical terms that must be translated precisely
. Imagine you have the English term
load balancer
and you’ve standardized on the German
Lastverteiler
. That’s not just a dictionary lookup; it has grammatical consequences:
term:
load balancer
status:
approved
targetLanguage:
de-DE
approvedTranslation:
Lastverteiler
part of speech (target):
noun
gender (target):
m
(der Lastverteiler)
usage context:
infrastructure product; use in technical docs and UI; avoid colloquial synonyms like "Load-Balancer"
A human translator now knows which article to use (
der Lastverteiler
, not
die
or
das
), how to inflect it in plural (
die Lastverteiler
), and that they shouldn’t improvise with near-synonyms. An AI system can be instructed: “When generating German, if you encounter
load balancer
, replace with
Lastverteiler
and apply masculine noun agreement.” That’s the difference between a translation that sounds native and one that feels slightly off to every German engineer who reads it.
Structured glossaries are also your best defense against
deprecated or risky terminology
. Every mature product accumulates legacy names and phrases you’d rather never see again. Maybe you renamed
Master Project
to
Primary Project
for inclusivity reasons. A spreadsheet might mark
Master Project
as “old term, don’t use,” but nothing stops it from slipping back into new content.
In a metadata-driven glossary, you’d define:
term:
Master Project
status:
deprecated
replacement:
Primary Project
usage context:
flag in all new content; allow only when quoting legacy UI or legal text
Now your translation QA can automatically flag any occurrence of
Master Project
in source or target. Human reviewers see a clear warning. AI agents can be told: “If you see a term with status
deprecated
, suggest the
replacement
term and add a comment.” You’re no longer relying on everyone remembering the latest brand guidelines; the system enforces them.
The real unlock comes when you treat this glossary not as a file, but as
shared infrastructure
. That means:
It lives in a database or terminology service, not in someone’s Downloads folder.
Your CMS, design system, and localization platform all read from the same source of truth.
Your AI prompts reference it explicitly: “Use the organization’s terminology API; never invent translations for terms with status
do-not-translate
or
approved
Your QA checks run against it automatically, flagging violations before content ships.
At that point, the glossary is no longer a document you send to vendors. It’s a runtime dependency of your content stack, just like your design tokens or feature flags.
This infrastructure mindset also changes how you
govern and evolve

terminology. Instead of a once-a-year cleanup of a bloated spreadsheet, you treat glossary changes like code changes: proposals, reviews, approvals, and versioning. When product marketing wants to introduce a new term, they don’t just update a slide deck; they create a glossary entry with status
experimental

, define usage context, and let localization leads review it for linguistic landmines in key markets. Once approved, that term becomes instantly available to every translator and AI agent through the same structured interface. You’ve built a feedback loop between brand, product, and localization, mediated by data, not email threads.

If you’re serious about translation quality—especially in a world where AI is doing more of the first pass—your glossary is no longer a nice-to-have reference. It’s part of your core infrastructure. Treat each entry as a tiny, well-modeled object: term, status, part of speech, gender, usage context, and any constraints that matter for your domain. Wire that into your tools so humans and machines are working from the same, enforced rules. The teams that do this stop arguing about “bad translations” and start shipping consistent, on-brand content in every language, at a speed that static spreadsheets simply can’t support.

title: Designing Locale-Specific Style Guides That Still Feel Like One Brand
excerpt: How to design per-locale style guides that respect cultural norms in Germany, Japan, and Brazil—without losing your core brand voice. Practical advice, concrete examples, and a simple structure you can reuse.

children.text: Most global brands underestimate how much
"professional" changes by market

. They roll out a single English-first tone-of-voice document, translate it, and then wonder why German customers find them vague, Japanese customers find them a bit rude, and Brazilian customers find them cold. The job of a locale-specific style guide is to
translate the brand, not just the words
: to encode formality, tone, and audience expectations in a way that preserves the brand’s core while letting it behave like a local.
When I build per-locale style guides, I start from a simple principle:
brand personality is global, behavior is local
. The personality might be "friendly, expert, optimistic" everywhere. But how that shows up in language is radically different. In Germany, "friendly and expert" often means
precise, structured, and formally respectful
. In Japan, it means
polite, indirect, and considerate of social distance
. In Brazil, it often means
warm, conversational, and emotionally open
. If your style guide doesn’t spell this out, your translators and local marketers will each improvise their own version—and your brand voice will fragment.
Take "professional" as a concrete example. In
Germany
, professional B2B copy usually leans on
Sie

-form, clear hierarchy, and technical precision. A global tagline like "We help you move faster" might become something like: "Wir unterstützen Sie dabei, Ihre Prozesse zu beschleunigen." The style guide should explicitly say:
Use Sie, not du, for all corporate and enterprise contexts; prefer precise, technical nouns over vague marketing language; avoid excessive enthusiasm or hyperbole.
A German reader will trust you more if you sound like an engineer who did their homework than a cheerleader.
In
Japan
, "professional" is less about technical density and more about
politeness and relational awareness
. The same tagline might become: 「お客様の業務プロセスを、よりスムーズに進められるようご支援いたします。」 Here, the style guide should specify:

Use desu/masu form; default to respectful, slightly indirect phrasing; avoid imperative forms that sound like you’re telling the customer what to do; show deference when talking about the customer’s business.

A brand that’s "friendly and casual" in English should be redefined in the Japanese guide as "friendly and respectful": approachable, but never over-familiar, and always careful about face and hierarchy.
In
Brazil
, "professional" often comfortably coexists with
warmth and informality
. For many consumer and even SMB contexts,
você
is standard, and a bit of colloquial phrasing can build trust. Our tagline might become: "Ajudamos você a acelerar seus processos." A Brazilian Portuguese style guide might say:
Use você as the default; keep sentences shorter and more conversational; allow light idioms and everyday vocabulary; show enthusiasm, but don’t oversell.

For some enterprise or government contexts, you might tighten the tone, but it will still likely be more approachable than the German equivalent. If your global brand is "friendly and casual" in English, the Brazilian guide can keep it close to literal: "amigável, próximo, direto ao ponto"—but still define where the line is between relaxed and sloppy.
This is where the
tension between brand consistency and cultural adaptation

shows up. If you enforce the same level of casualness everywhere, you’ll sound unprofessional in Tokyo and oddly stiff in São Paulo. If you let every market decide tone ad hoc, you’ll end up with what I’ve seen too many times: a German site that reads like a legal contract, a Japanese site that sounds like a government notice, and a Brazilian site that feels like a party—all under the same logo. The way out is to define
non-negotiables
(what must stay the same) and
flexible dimensions
(what can adapt) in each locale’s style guide.
A practical way to do this is to make each locale-specific guide a short, opinionated document with four core sections:
Formality setting, Tone adjectives, Audience description, and Additional instructions.

For formality, be explicit: in German, "Use Sie for all external communications except social media replies to individuals under 30, where du may be acceptable if they use it first." In Japanese, "Use desu/masu form in all customer-facing content; avoid overly casual forms even on social media." In Brazilian Portuguese, "Use você in all B2C and most B2B contexts; reserve more formal constructions for legal, HR, and investor materials." Don’t leave this to translator intuition—write it down.
For
tone adjectives
, I like to define 3–5 core words per locale, mapped back to the global brand. For example, if the global brand is "friendly, expert, straightforward":
Germany:
freundlich, kompetent, präzise
(friendly, competent, precise) – note how "straightforward" becomes "präzise" rather than "direkt", which can feel blunt.
Japan:
親しみやすい, 信頼できる, 丁寧
(approachable, trustworthy, polite) – "straightforward" is expressed through clarity and structure, not bluntness.
Brazil:
amigável, especialista, direto
(friendly, expert, direct) – here "direct" is closer to the English intent and culturally acceptable.
Spell out what each adjective
does and does not
mean in that market, with examples of good and bad sentences.
The
audience description

section should be written like a mini creative brief for that locale. Who are we talking to, and what do they expect from a brand like ours? For Germany, you might note: "Primary audience: mid-career professionals in engineering and operations roles; value clarity, evidence, and reliability; skeptical of exaggerated claims." For Japan: "Primary audience: managers in large organizations; value harmony, risk avoidance, and social proof; prefer modest claims backed by case studies." For Brazil: "Primary audience: entrepreneurs and managers in growing businesses; value agility, personal connection, and optimism; respond well to stories and concrete benefits." This context helps writers understand
why
the formality and tone rules exist, so they can make smart trade-offs when the guide doesn’t cover a specific edge case.
Finally, the
additional instructions

section is where the hard-won, market-specific wisdom lives. This is where you capture things like: "Germany: Avoid English buzzwords where a good German term exists; structure long pages with clear headings and numbered lists; include data points where possible." "Japan: Avoid direct criticism of competitors; use softeners like ～と考えています or ～かもしれません when presenting opinions; be careful with humor, and avoid sarcasm entirely." "Brazil: It’s acceptable to use rhetorical questions in headlines; emojis may be used sparingly in social content but not in formal emails; avoid overly literal translations of US cultural references." The best guides I’ve seen also include
before/after examples
: a literal translation that feels off, and a culturally tuned version that still reflects the brand.
If you’re starting from scratch, don’t aim for a 40-page manual. Aim for a
5–8 page, highly practical document per locale

that a busy marketer or translator can actually read and use. Start with the four sections above, add 5–10 example sentences (headlines, CTAs, email openings) that show the voice in action, and then iterate with local teams. Ask them: "Where does this feel too foreign? Where does it feel too generic?" Over time, your locale-specific style guides become not just documentation, but a shared contract: a way to let your brand speak like a local
without ever forgetting who it is.

title: Structured Metadata Is What Makes AI Translation Actually Work
excerpt: Raw AI translation is impressive, but it’s not production-ready on its own. The real gains show up when you wrap models in structured metadata: glossaries, style guides, and content-aware filtering that assemble the right prompt for each locale and each piece of content.

children.text: Most teams discover the same thing the hard way: AI translation looks magical in a demo and painfully unreliable in production. Pasting copy into ChatGPT or a generic translation UI is fine for a one-off email, but it falls apart when you’re localizing a product, a website, or a multi-market campaign. The issue isn’t that the models are bad; it’s that the inputs are unstructured. Without metadata, the model has to guess your terminology, your tone, and your intent. And models are very confident guessers.

The gap between “raw AI translation” and “production-grade localization” is almost entirely a metadata problem. Once you treat translation as a structured pipeline—where glossaries, style guides, and locale rules are assembled into the prompt at translation time—the quality jump is dramatic. You go from arguing about weird word choices in every review cycle to arguing about far more interesting things, like which terms should be in the glossary in the first place.

Let’s start with glossaries. In theory, you can tell a model, “Please keep product names in English and use our approved terminology.” In practice, that instruction gets ignored as soon as the surrounding context is ambiguous. I’ve seen a B2B SaaS product called “Flow” translated into French as
Flux
in half the UI and left as
Flow
in the other half. The model wasn’t wrong linguistically, but it was wrong for the brand. A structured glossary solves this by turning brand and product names into explicit metadata:
Flow → Flow (do not translate)
Workspace → Espace de travail
Seat → Licence utilisateur

When that glossary is wired into the translation pipeline, the model never has to guess. Every time it sees “Flow” in the source, the prompt includes an instruction like: “Use the following approved terms exactly as specified: Flow (do not translate), Workspace → Espace de travail, Seat → Licence utilisateur.” The result is boringly consistent—which is exactly what you want. No more launch-week surprises where your flagship feature has three different names across your help center, UI, and marketing site.

Glossaries also prevent subtle, high-stakes mistranslations. One real example: a fintech company had an internal product called “Vault.” In English, that’s clearly a secure storage concept. In some languages, the model happily translated it to a generic “safe” or “locker,” which sounded like a physical object, not a digital service. Legal and compliance teams were not amused. Once we encoded
Vault → Vault (branded product, do not translate)
into the glossary and enforced it in the pipeline, those issues disappeared. The model still handled the surrounding sentence naturally, but the brand term stayed locked down.

This is the difference between hoping the model respects your brand and guaranteeing it. Raw AI translation treats every token as fair game. A glossary-backed pipeline treats certain tokens as contractually non-negotiable. If you care about trademarks, regulated language, or even just not confusing your customers, that’s not optional—it’s table stakes.

Then there’s style. People underestimate how much tone and register matter until they see a German UI that sounds like a legal notice or a Spanish marketing email that reads like a government form. Models are good at “sound professional” or “be friendly,” but those are vague, anglo-centric instructions. A production-grade system uses locale-specific style guides as metadata: for German,
use "Sie" in enterprise contexts, "du" in SMB/self-serve; avoid Anglicisms in legal copy; keep button labels short and imperative.
For Japanese,
avoid overly casual forms in B2B; prefer polite but concise constructions; keep English product names in Latin script.
In a structured pipeline, that style guidance isn’t a one-off note you paste into the prompt when you remember. It’s a first-class object:
style_guide.de-enterprise
style_guide.es-consumer
style_guide.ja-b2b

. At translation time, the agent assembles the right one based on metadata on the content: product area, audience, channel, and locale. The model doesn’t just translate; it localizes into the correct voice for that specific scenario.

I’ve seen the opposite approach too many times: someone pastes a batch of strings into ChatGPT with a prompt like, “Translate this into German in a friendly but professional tone.” The output looks fine to the English-speaking PM, but the German team flags it as inconsistent with their brand voice, too casual in the legal pages, and too formal in the onboarding emails. You end up in a loop where native speakers rewrite half the content, defeating the whole point of automation.
Once you encode style rules as metadata, you can be opinionated and precise:
For German marketing emails: use "du"; keep subject lines under 50 characters; avoid literal translations of English idioms; prefer these sample phrases.

The model now has concrete constraints and examples, not vibes. Over time, you refine the style guides based on feedback, and every future translation benefits automatically. That’s how you get compounding quality improvements instead of re-litigating tone on every project.

The third piece—content-aware filtering—is where most teams quietly sabotage themselves. They build a massive global glossary and then dump the entire thing into every translation prompt. The model gets a wall of terms, half of which are irrelevant to the current text. Best case, it ignores most of them. Worst case, it starts forcing in the wrong terms because they look statistically related.

Imagine you’re translating a help article about billing. Your global glossary includes product names, HR terms, legal boilerplate, and marketing taglines. If you shove all of that into the prompt, the model might decide that “Plan” should always be translated as your branded “Subscription Plan” term, even when the sentence is clearly about a generic plan or idea. Or it might over-apply a marketing tagline translation in a technical context. The problem isn’t the glossary; it’s the lack of filtering.
A structured pipeline does something smarter: it uses the content metadata to select only the relevant glossary entries for each translation job. If the content is tagged as
domain: billing
and
surface: help-center
, the agent pulls in only the billing-related terms and the help-center style guide. If it’s
domain: product-ui
and
surface: mobile-app
, it uses the UI-specific glossary and the mobile tone rules. The prompt stays focused, and the model isn’t distracted by terms that don’t belong.

You can get quite granular here. For example, you might have different approved translations for the same English term depending on context: “Workspace” in the UI vs. “Workspace” in legal docs. With content-aware filtering, the pipeline knows which variant to inject because the content itself carries the necessary tags. This is nearly impossible to manage reliably when humans are copy-pasting text into a generic chat interface.

This is where the “paste text into ChatGPT” approach really shows its limits. It treats translation as an ad hoc conversation instead of a repeatable process. Every human operator has to remember the right glossary, the right tone, the right locale quirks, and then manually cram that into a prompt. People get tired, rushed, or pulled into meetings. Prompts get shortened, glossaries get skipped, and style rules get half-applied. The model output reflects that inconsistency.

In a structured pipeline, the operator doesn’t improvise the prompt; the system assembles it. The source text comes in with metadata—locale, domain, channel, brand, audience. The pipeline resolves that into a specific combination of glossaries, style guides, and locale rules, then builds a deterministic prompt for the agent. The human’s job shifts from “remember everything” to “curate and improve the metadata.” That’s a much more scalable use of expert time.

I’ve watched teams move from copy-paste workflows to metadata-driven pipelines, and the pattern is consistent. At first, there’s skepticism: “Do we really need all this structure? The model seems pretty good out of the box.” Then they hit their first big release with 10+ locales, and the review burden explodes. Brand names drift, tone is all over the place, and reviewers start building their own unofficial glossaries in spreadsheets. That’s usually the moment when someone admits that the problem isn’t the model—it’s the lack of system.

Once they invest in structured metadata, two things happen. Quality goes up because the model is finally operating with the same constraints humans use. And speed goes up because reviewers are no longer fixing the same terminology and tone issues on every project. Instead, they update the glossary or style guide once, and the pipeline enforces it everywhere. The AI stops being a clever toy and starts behaving like a disciplined, opinionated translator that actually understands the business.

If you’re serious about using AI for translation, the question isn’t “Which model is best?” It’s “How much structure are we willing to put around the model?” Raw AI translation will always be impressive in a vacuum. But production-grade output—the kind you can ship to millions of users without flinching—comes from structured metadata: glossaries that lock in terminology, style guides that encode voice per locale, and content-aware filtering that keeps prompts lean and relevant.

The teams that win here treat metadata as a product, not an afterthought. They invest in building and maintaining glossaries, codifying style, and tagging content accurately. The model is just the engine. Structured metadata is the steering wheel, the brakes, and the dashboard. Without it, you’re going fast—but you’re not really in control.

title: The Content Reuse Playbook for Multi‑Market Teams
excerpt: How to structure content, workflows, and ownership so global teams can translate, transcreate, and create locally without breaking the source.

children.text: If you want real content reuse across markets, you have to stop thinking in terms of pages and campaigns, and start thinking in terms of
content assets and systems

. The teams that struggle the most are the ones trying to copy/paste English pages into other languages and hoping for consistency. The teams that win treat content like a product: they design it, model it, and govern it so local markets can adapt it without breaking the source. That means getting very clear on what gets translated, what gets transcreated, and what must be created locally from scratch.
Broadly, you have three buckets. First,
content that should be translated

: product descriptions, feature lists, technical specs, help center articles, API docs, legal notices. This content is about accuracy, completeness, and consistency. If your US product page says the phone has a 6.7" OLED display and IP68 rating, your German page needs to say exactly that—no more, no less. Here, reuse is about having a single source of truth and pushing that into every market with minimal variation. You can still localize units, date formats, and examples, but the underlying facts must stay aligned.
Second,
content that should be transcreated

: brand campaigns, hero copy, value propositions, email subject lines, social ads, video scripts. This is where literal translation kills performance. I’ve seen a global tagline that crushed it in English (“Work without limits”) flop in Japan because the phrase implied recklessness and overwork. The Japanese team didn’t need a translation; they needed a culturally resonant idea that still expressed the same brand promise. Transcreation means you preserve the
intent, emotion, and strategic message
, but you give local teams permission to change the words, references, and even formats to fit their market.
Third,
content that should be created locally

: market-specific case studies, local regulatory explanations, pricing pages where models differ, local partnership announcements, and anything tied to local culture or policy. A B2B SaaS platform selling into Germany needs case studies about German manufacturers and references to German data protection law; a copy of a US fintech case study won’t cut it. Likewise, HR policy content for France (with its specific labor laws) can’t be a translation of US HR content. Here, reuse is more about
reusing structure and patterns
than reusing the actual words.
This is where
structured content models
do the heavy lifting. Instead of a big blob of page HTML, you break content into fields and components:
product_name
short_description
feature_list
legal_disclaimer
hero_headline
supporting_points
cta_label
case_study_industry
case_study_metrics

, and so on. You separate content from presentation: the CMS stores the content; your front end decides how it looks. That lets you say, for example, “these fields are global and translated; these fields are local and created in-market; these fields are transcreated with guardrails.”
A practical example: for a global product detail page, you might define a model where
technical_specs
dimensions
, and
compatibility
are
global reference fields
—authored once, then translated. Local markets can’t edit the source; they can only provide language variants. Meanwhile,
benefit_headline
benefit_body
, and
social_proof_quote
are
market-owned fields
: each market can write their own copy, but they still use the same structure. Finally,
brand_promise
and
core_value_prop
might be
transcreation fields
: they inherit a global intent description (e.g., “Convey that we remove complexity from IT workflows”), plus do/don’t examples, and local teams write their own version within that frame.
References instead of duplication are non‑negotiable. If your help center article about “Resetting your password” exists in 30 markets, you don’t want 30 separate, free-floating articles. You want
one canonical article

with language variants referenced wherever it appears: in-app help, web help center, chatbot responses. When the security team updates the password policy, the global article changes once, and all locales inherit the update. Local teams can still add
market-specific callouts
in dedicated fields (e.g., “In Brazil, SMS codes may be delayed due to carrier restrictions”), but they’re not forking the entire article.
The organizational piece is where most teams fail. You need
clear ownership and workflows

baked into your tools. At minimum: a global content team owns the source models and global content; regional leads own transcreation; local market teams own local-only content. Your CMS workflow should reflect this: global content moves from Draft → Review → Approved, then triggers localization tasks. Translators and local marketers work in
language variants of the same entry
, not in cloned copies. They can propose changes, but structural changes (adding/removing fields, changing component types) are controlled centrally so no one breaks the schema.

In practice, this looks like: a global campaign brief is created in the CMS as a structured object—objective, audience, key message, mandatory elements, banned claims, example copy. Each market gets a task to create their own
market_campaign_variant

entry that references the global brief. They can change headlines, visuals, and channels, but they can’t change the core claim or legal line because those are referenced from the global object. If a regulator forces a change to the claim in one market, you either update the global object (and all markets inherit) or you add a
market override field
with a clear audit trail.
The last mile is governance and measurement. Define
which content types follow which path

(translate vs transcreate vs local-create) and codify that in your playbook and tooling. Don’t rely on tribal knowledge. Use content types, field-level permissions, and workflows to enforce it. Then measure: how often are markets forking content? How many local variants exist for a single concept? How long does it take to roll out a global change? The goal isn’t to lock markets down; it’s to give them
structured freedom

—enough flexibility to be relevant locally, with enough structure that you can still operate like one brand. When you get that balance right, reuse stops being a cost-saving tactic and becomes a strategic advantage.

title: How to Run Seasonal Campaigns Across Cultures Without Losing Your Mind (or Your ROI)
excerpt: Seasonal and holiday campaigns get messy fast once you move beyond a single country. "Back to School" happens in August in the US but April in Japan, Black Friday means different things in different markets, and some of the biggest shopping moments—like Singles Day, Diwali, or Golden Week—barely register elsewhere. This article breaks down the operational and cultural challenges of running seasonal campaigns across markets and offers a practical framework for building a global seasonal content calendar that actually works.

children.text: Running seasonal campaigns across multiple markets is where otherwise well-run marketing teams quietly bleed money. The problem isn’t creativity; it’s orchestration. The moment you move beyond a single country, your neat Q3 “Back to School” plan collides with the reality that school starts in August in the US, September in parts of Europe, and April in Japan. Add in Black Friday’s uneven cultural relevance, market-specific events like Singles Day in China, Diwali in India, and Golden Week in Japan, and you’re suddenly running a global air-traffic control tower for content, not a simple campaign calendar.
The first hard lesson:
seasonality is local, not global

. In the US, “Back to School” is a late-July to early-September story: backpacks, laptops, dorm room setups, and “new year, new you” messaging for parents and students. In Japan, the school year starts in April, so the emotional context is different: cherry blossoms, fresh starts, and a more formal, almost ceremonial tone. If you simply translate your US August creative into Japanese and run it in April, it will feel off. The visual language (colors, uniforms, family dynamics), the timing (salary bonus cycles, paydays), and even the hero products (stationery vs. tech) need to be rethought, not just reworded.

Black Friday is another trap. In the US, it’s a cultural event: doorbusters, midnight openings (now mostly online), and a very specific “deal frenzy” tone. In the UK and parts of Europe, Black Friday exists but is more of an imported retail construct—customers are aware, but the emotional weight is lighter. In markets like Japan or India, Black Friday may be recognized by e-commerce platforms but doesn’t carry the same social ritual. I’ve seen global teams push aggressive “Black Friday” creative into markets where it felt like a random sale with a foreign name. The better approach is to treat Black Friday as
one of several Q4 value moments
, and let each market decide whether to lean into it, reframe it (e.g., “November Mega Sale”), or deprioritize it in favor of more relevant events.
Then there are
market-specific holidays that are absolutely massive locally and invisible elsewhere

. Singles Day (11.11) in China is the obvious example: it’s not just a sale, it’s an infrastructure event—logistics, payments, and media all bend around it. Diwali in India is similar: a multi-week festival season where gifting, home improvement, and big-ticket purchases spike, but the emotional narrative is about light, family, and renewal. Golden Week in Japan is a cluster of holidays that drives travel, leisure, and home spending. If your global calendar is built around US/European anchors and you treat these as “nice-to-have local promos,” you’re leaving serious revenue on the table and frustrating local teams who know these are their Super Bowl moments.
The content operations challenge is usually underestimated. To do this well, you’re not just translating copy; you’re
rebuilding creative systems

. That means preparing campaign assets months in advance so local teams have time to adapt: rewriting headlines, swapping imagery (e.g., clothing styles, family compositions, food, colors with cultural meaning), and adjusting tone (hard-sell vs. aspirational vs. community-focused). A “Back to School” hero image of a US suburban family in a minivan doesn’t land in central Tokyo; a Diwali visual with candles and rangoli patterns might need careful adaptation for markets where fire imagery is regulated or misunderstood. If you don’t bake localization time into your production schedule, you force markets to either ship off-brand or miss the moment entirely.

Timezones add another layer of operational pain. When you’re coordinating a Singles Day campaign across China, Southeast Asia, and global .com, launch timing isn’t just “midnight local.” You’re dealing with platform-specific windows (e.g., pre-sale periods on marketplaces), staggered email sends, and support coverage. I’ve seen global teams schedule a “global” Black Friday email based on US Eastern Time, only to hit Europe in the middle of the night and APAC at the wrong point in their day. The fix is boring but critical:
build campaign runbooks that specify timing by market and channel
, and centralize a single source of truth (often a shared calendar plus a run-of-show doc) that local teams can trust.
A practical way to tame this complexity is to build a
Global Seasonal Content Framework
with four layers: (1)
Global Moments
Regional Moments
Local Moments
, and (4)
Always-On Themes

. Global Moments are the truly shared anchors—e.g., New Year, Q4 gifting, or a global product launch—where you drive a consistent narrative and visual identity, with local nuance. Regional Moments might be things like European summer holidays or Ramadan across MENA and parts of Southeast Asia. Local Moments are your Singles Day, Diwali, Golden Week, local back-to-school, national days, and local shopping festivals. Always-On Themes are cross-cutting narratives like “learning,” “home comfort,” or “self-care” that you can dial up around different local events without reinventing the brand each time.
Operationally, I recommend a
rolling 12–18 month seasonal calendar
built in three passes:
Pass 1 – Strategic Mapping (12–18 months out):
Central team and local leads map all known seasonal events into the four layers. For each market, you mark events as
Primary
Secondary
, or
Monitored

(no planned activation, but keep an eye on it). This is where you align on which markets treat Black Friday as Primary vs. Secondary, and where Singles Day, Diwali, Golden Week, and local Back to School sit.
Pass 2 – Campaign Architecture (6–9 months out):

For each Primary event, define the campaign concept, objectives, and a modular asset system: core hero visuals, flexible layouts, copy frameworks, and content pillars. You’re not writing every line of copy yet, but you’re deciding what can be standardized and what must be localized (e.g., offer mechanics, cultural references, imagery).
Pass 3 – Localization & Production (3–4 months out):

Local teams receive the campaign kit and adapt it: local messaging, visuals, channel mix, and timing. Central ops tracks dependencies (e.g., product availability, legal approvals, marketplace requirements) and ensures there’s enough buffer for QA and last-minute changes.
To make this work in practice, you need
clear governance and tooling

. At minimum: a shared calendar (even a well-structured spreadsheet) that lists each event by market with start/end dates, priority, and owner; a content management or DAM system where global assets and local adaptations live; and a simple but enforced workflow for approvals. For example, global might own the master “Back to School” narrative and design system, while Japan owns the April execution, including local imagery and channel strategy. Global reviews for brand and legal; Japan decides what will actually convert. The teams that win at seasonal marketing across cultures aren’t the ones with the flashiest creative—they’re the ones that respect local seasonality, plan far enough ahead for real localization, and run content operations like a disciplined product organization, not a last-minute art project.

title: Building Translation Evaluation Frameworks That Actually Prove Quality
excerpt: How to design measurable, repeatable evaluation frameworks for translation quality using deterministic checks, LLM-as-judge scoring, and structured baselines that prove the value of glossaries and style guides.

children.text: Most teams still evaluate translations with a shrug and a gut check: “does it sound right?” That works when you have a small volume, a single language, and a heroic reviewer who knows the product inside out. It completely breaks down when you’re shipping weekly to 15 locales, mixing human and machine translation, and trying to justify budget for terminology work or style guides. At that point, you don’t need vibes; you need an evaluation framework that produces numbers you can track, defend, and improve against.
When I help teams build these frameworks, we start by separating two layers:
deterministic checks
(things that are objectively true or false) and
judgment-based scoring

(things that require linguistic or contextual interpretation). Deterministic checks are your foundation: glossary term presence, forbidden term absence, formality markers, punctuation and number formats, structural constraints (e.g., placeholders preserved). Judgment-based scoring—often using LLMs-as-judges—sits on top and evaluates dimensions like fluency, accuracy, and style adherence. The magic happens when you wire both layers into an automated pipeline and run them across controlled baselines.

Deterministic checks are where you get your first real leverage, because they’re cheap to automate and hard to argue with. Suppose your German glossary says “workspace” must always be translated as “Arbeitsbereich,” and “Space” (the product name) must remain untranslated. You can write a simple checker that scans the target text for required terms and flags disallowed variants like “Arbeitsraum” or “Bereich.” Similarly, you can enforce forbidden terms—maybe legal has banned “unlimited” in all EU locales, or marketing doesn’t want “cheap” in any language. These checks don’t tell you if the translation is beautiful, but they do tell you if it’s compliant.

Formality and register are another area where deterministic signals go a long way. In languages like German, Spanish, or French, you can codify whether a product or brand should use formal or informal address. For German, that might mean counting occurrences of “Sie/Ihnen/Ihr” vs. “du/dir/dein,” and setting a threshold (e.g., 95%+ of second-person pronouns must match the chosen register). For Japanese, you might check for specific politeness markers or verb forms. These rules won’t catch every nuance, but they turn “this feels too casual” into a measurable, trackable metric:
formality adherence rate
Once the basics are enforced deterministically, you can layer in
LLM-as-judge
evaluations to score higher-order qualities. The pattern that works best in practice is to define a rubric with 3–5 dimensions—commonly:
accuracy
fluency
style adherence
, and sometimes
terminology
and
format/structure

. For each dimension, you define a 1–5 scale with concrete descriptors. For example, accuracy might be: 5 = fully faithful, no meaning errors; 4 = minor nuance differences; 3 = one noticeable but non-critical error; 2 = multiple or serious errors; 1 = misleading or wrong. You then prompt the LLM with the source, the translation, the rubric, and any relevant context (glossary, style guide summary), and ask it to output structured scores plus a short justification per dimension.
The key to making LLM-as-judge useful is
consistency and constraints

. Use the same model, the same prompt template, and the same rubric across runs. Force the model to output machine-readable JSON so you can aggregate scores. For example, for a marketing headline you might ask: “Score fluency, accuracy, and style adherence from 1–5 based on the rubric below. Style adherence should focus on tone (playful vs. serious), sentence length, and avoidance of banned phrases.” Over time, you’ll see patterns: maybe your MT engine is consistently strong on accuracy (4.3 average) but weak on style adherence in Japanese (2.7 average). That’s a signal to invest in style-specific tuning or post-editing guidelines for that locale.
To
prove the value of structured context

—glossaries, style guides, product descriptions—you should design explicit baseline experiments. Take a representative sample of content (say, 200 segments across product UI, help center, and marketing emails). Translate them twice: once with no extra context (“raw MT” or “no instructions”), and once with structured context injected (glossary terms, tone-of-voice summary, target audience, examples). Run both sets through the same deterministic checks and LLM-as-judge rubric. If your glossary is doing its job, you should see terminology adherence jump from, say, 72% to 96%, and style adherence scores rise by at least a full point in key locales. Those deltas are what you bring to leadership when you argue for the time spent maintaining glossaries and style guides.

Automating this into a pipeline is mostly plumbing. At a minimum, you want: (1) a way to sample or select test strings (e.g., from your TMS or codebase), (2) a translation step (MT engine or vendor API), (3) a deterministic checker (could be a simple script or a QA tool like Xbench or custom regex-based validators), and (4) an evaluation step that calls your LLM-as-judge with a fixed prompt and collects scores. Wrap it in a scheduled job (nightly or per-release), store results in a database or spreadsheet, and visualize trends in a dashboard. The first version can be ugly; what matters is that it runs without human intervention and produces comparable numbers over time.

Choosing scoring rubrics is where teams often overcomplicate things. Start simple and aligned to decisions you actually need to make. If you’re deciding whether MT is good enough for internal support docs, you might only care about
accuracy
and
readability
(fluency). If you’re evaluating translations for brand campaigns, you’ll care more about
style adherence
and
emotional impact

, and you might accept slightly lower literal accuracy. Keep scales coarse (1–5 or 1–3) and descriptions concrete. Avoid rubrics that require native-level literary criticism; your goal is operational guidance, not academic purity. You can always refine the rubric after a few runs when you see where the scores cluster.
Evaluating across languages where you don’t have fluent reviewers is where LLM-as-judge and deterministic checks really shine, but you still need guardrails. One effective pattern is
triangulation

: use an LLM that’s strong in the target language to score the translation, then separately back-translate the target into the source language and have another LLM (or the same one with a different prompt) compare the back-translation to the original for meaning drift. Combine that with deterministic checks for placeholders, numbers, and glossary terms. You won’t catch every subtle cultural misstep, but you’ll dramatically reduce the risk of outright meaning errors or broken UI strings in languages where you lack in-house expertise.
The last piece is interpreting results in a way that drives action rather than blame. Don’t obsess over single scores; look at
patterns over time and by segment type

. Maybe your overall fluency is fine, but error rates spike in error messages or legal disclaimers. Maybe Japanese and Korean lag behind European languages on style adherence because your style guide is Euro-centric. Use the data to prioritize: which locales need human review for which content types, where glossaries are under-specified, where MT prompts need more context. The goal of an evaluation framework isn’t to declare “good” or “bad”; it’s to create a feedback loop that steadily moves your translations from “sounds okay” to “consistently on-brand, on-message, and provably high quality.”

title: Structured Content as the Engine of Multi‑Channel Delivery
excerpt: Separating content from presentation is no longer a nice-to-have—it’s the only sustainable way to deliver consistent messaging across web, mobile, email, print, and emerging channels. Here’s what it really takes to make structured content work in the real world.

children.text: Most teams discover the value of structured content the hard way: the third time they rewrite the same product announcement for the website, the email campaign, the app banner, and the sales one-pager. Each version drifts a little, legal catches different issues in different places, and by launch day no one is sure which wording is actually approved. The root problem isn’t copywriting; it’s architecture. If content and presentation are fused together, every channel becomes a separate content universe. The only way to deliver consistent messaging at scale is to separate
what
you say (content) from
how
and
where
it appears (presentation).

When we talk about structured content, we’re talking about modeling content as data: discrete fields and objects with meaning, not just blobs of HTML or WYSIWYG output. A product announcement stops being a single page and becomes a set of fields like
headline
subheadline
summary
body
key_benefits[]
release_date
audience
, and
cta

. Rich text formats like Portable Text push this further by storing the body copy itself as structured data—blocks, spans, annotations, and embedded objects—rather than pre-rendered markup. That means the same underlying content can be rendered as a long-form article on the web, a condensed snippet in email, or a tight notification in your app, all without duplicating or re-authoring the message.

Portable Text is a good example of how this works in practice. Instead of saving a blog post as a single HTML string, you store an array of blocks: paragraphs, headings, lists, images, quotes, and even custom objects like
product_callout
or
feature_grid
. Each block has a type and attributes, and inline spans can carry marks like
strong
em
, or
link

as data. On the web, you might render that as semantic HTML with rich styling. In a mobile app, you map the same blocks to native components. For email, you flatten or simplify certain blocks to avoid layout issues. For print, you feed the structure into a template that respects brand typography and spacing. The content doesn’t change; only the presentation layer does.

This shift has a profound organizational impact: content teams have to stop thinking in terms of “pages” and start thinking in terms of “content objects.” A page is a destination; a content object is a reusable unit that can flow into many destinations. In a page-centric mindset, you brief writers to “create a launch page for Product X.” In a structured-content mindset, you brief them to “create the canonical Product X announcement object,” which includes all the fields and variants needed to support web, email, social, in-app, and sales enablement. The CMS stops being a page builder and becomes a content repository with clear models, relationships, and lifecycle states.
Consider a single product announcement as a concrete example. In a structured model, you might have:
Core fields
title
short_title
summary
body
release_date
product_name
tier
audience_segment
Messaging fields
value_prop
problem_statement
key_benefits[]
quote_from_PM
quote_from_customer
Channel-specific helpers
social_caption
email_subject
email_preheader
in_app_title
in_app_body
seo_title
seo_description
All of this lives in a single source document. Portable Text powers the
body
and maybe
key_benefits[]

so you can embed rich elements (like screenshots or comparison tables) where appropriate. The important part is that every channel pulls from this same object, with clear rules about which fields to use and how to transform them.
From that one announcement object, you can render a
full blog post
on the website by using
title
summary
, and the full Portable Text
body
, plus embedded product callouts and images. The
social media card
might use
short_title
or a trimmed
value_prop
for the headline,
social_caption
for the copy, and a selected hero image. Your
email snippet
can combine
email_subject
email_preheader
, and a condensed version of the
summary
or first few Portable Text blocks, automatically truncated to a safe length. The
in-app notification
might use
in_app_title
and
in_app_body
, with strict character limits enforced at the model level, and a deep link that’s derived from the canonical slug. Four very different surfaces, one underlying source of truth.
The real payoff shows up over time. When legal needs a wording change—say, a claim in the value proposition is too strong—you update the
value_prop

field once. The next time the website renders the blog post, the app shows the notification, or the CRM system pulls content for a nurture email, they all get the corrected copy. When you localize, you’re not asking translators to hunt through five different “pages”; they translate the fields on the announcement object, and every channel benefits. Analytics also become more meaningful: you can track how a specific
key_benefit
performs across channels, or A/B test
email_subject
variants while keeping the core message stable.

None of this happens by accident. Moving to structured content is as much an organizational shift as a technical one. You need content strategists who can design content models, not just page layouts. You need writers who are comfortable authoring into fields and blocks instead of a single freeform canvas. You need engineers who treat content as data and build renderers for each channel, rather than hard-coding copy into templates. And you need governance: naming conventions, reuse guidelines, and clear ownership of canonical objects. The teams that make this leap stop fighting fires at launch time and start operating like a product organization for content—shipping consistent, reusable, multi-channel messaging from a single, well-structured source.

title: So orchestrieren Sie einen gleichzeitigen Produktlaunch in 12 Märkten
excerpt: Ein globaler Launch, bei dem in 12 Märkten innerhalb von 24 Stunden alles gleichzeitig live geht, ist kein Marketing-Stunt, sondern ein logistisches Großprojekt. Dieser Leitfaden zeigt praxisnah, wie Sie Zeitzonen, Übersetzungen, Regulierung, Lokalisierung und Content-Strukturen so aufsetzen, dass der Go-live nicht im Chaos endet.

children.text: Warum ein simultaner Launch mehr ist als „nur“ gutes Timing

Ein Produkt gleichzeitig in 12 Märkten zu launchen klingt glamourös – in der Praxis ist es vor allem eins: brutal komplex. Sie jonglieren Zeitzonen, rechtliche Freigaben, Übersetzungen, lokale Kampagnen, App-Store-Updates, Support-Playbooks und interne Kommunikation. Und alles muss in einem 24-Stunden-Fenster fehlerfrei zusammenlaufen.

Ich habe Launches erlebt, bei denen wir in Europa schon gefeiert haben, während in Lateinamerika noch die Regulatorik blockierte und in Asien veraltete Screenshots live waren. Der Unterschied zwischen Chaos und kontrollierter Präzision liegt selten in „mehr Aufwand“, sondern in Struktur: Wie Sie Content modellieren, wie Sie Handoffs über Zeitzonen organisieren und wie früh Sie Lokalisierung und Legal einbinden.
Dieser Artikel beschreibt einen Ansatz, der sich in mehreren globalen Launches bewährt hat – inklusive der Fehler, die Sie besser nicht wiederholen.
Von der Zeitzonen-Hölle zur 24-Stunden-Choreografie
Der erste Denkfehler bei globalen Launches: „Wir gehen einfach um 10 Uhr CET live.“ Global bedeutet nicht „Europa plus ein bisschen Rest“. Sie brauchen ein klares Referenz-Zeitmodell.
Bewährt hat sich, alles auf
UTC
zu planen und für jedes Workstream ein
Follow-the-sun-Playbook
zu definieren:
T-24 bis T-12 (APAC-Shift)
: Letzte technische Checks, Feature-Flags setzen, Staging-Umgebungen einfrieren. Lokale Teams in APAC prüfen Lokalisierung und regulatorische Hinweise.
T-12 bis T-0 (EMEA-Shift)
: Zentrale Orchestrierung, finale Freigaben, Aktivierung von Kampagnen, Social und PR. EMEA trägt die Hauptverantwortung für den globalen Status.
T-0 bis T+12 (Americas-Shift)
: Monitoring, Incident-Response, Performance-Optimierung, Social-Media-Reaktionen, Paid-Kampagnen feinjustieren.
Wichtig:
Jede Zeitzone hat einen klar benannten „Incident Commander“

mit Entscheidungshoheit. Nichts ist schlimmer, als nachts um 3 Uhr in Berlin Slack-Nachrichten aus Kalifornien zu lesen wie „Dürfen wir das jetzt fixen oder nicht?“. Legen Sie vorab fest, welche Entscheidungen lokal getroffen werden dürfen und welche global abgestimmt werden müssen.
Übersetzung ist nicht Lokalisierung – und schon gar nicht bei Produktnamen

Ein simultaner Launch scheitert oft an Details, die man für „Kleinkram“ hält. Klassisches Beispiel: Produktnamen. Was auf Englisch frisch und modern klingt, kann in einer anderen Sprache peinlich oder sogar beleidigend sein.

Ich habe einmal einen Fintech-Feature-Namen erlebt, der im Deutschen harmlos war, im Spanischen aber umgangssprachlich für „Betrug“ stand. Die Kampagne war fertig, die Videos produziert – und wir mussten 10 Tage vor Launch in drei Märkten umbenennen.
Lernen daraus:
Namens-Check frühzeitig einplanen

: Spätestens, wenn ein Name in die engere Auswahl kommt, lassen Sie ihn von Muttersprachler:innen in allen Zielmärkten prüfen – nicht nur von Übersetzungsagenturen, sondern von Menschen, die die Alltagssprache kennen.
Lokale Freiheiten zulassen

: Es ist völlig in Ordnung, wenn ein Produkt in Japan einen anderen Namen trägt als in Deutschland, solange die Markenarchitektur klar bleibt. Dokumentieren Sie diese Varianten strukturiert im Content-Modell.
Transcreation statt Übersetzung
: Headlines, Claims und Key Messages sollten nicht „übersetzt“, sondern
neu geschrieben
werden – mit derselben Intention, aber lokalem Sprachgefühl.
Im Content-Modell bedeutet das: Statt einem Feld
productName
brauchen Sie z. B.
productNameGlobal
plus
productNameLocalized[de, fr, es, ja,…]
– mit klaren Regeln, wann welche Variante wo verwendet wird.
Übersetzungs-Pipelines: Weg vom E-Mail-Pingpong
Wer für 12 Märkte launcht und noch mit Excel-Listen und E-Mail-Anhängen arbeitet, hat den Launch schon verloren. Sie brauchen eine
Übersetzungs-Pipeline
, die zu Ihrem Content-Modell passt.
Ein erprobtes Setup:
Zentrale Content-Erstellung im Sanity Studio
: Alle Master-Texte (Englisch oder Deutsch, je nach Organisation) werden als strukturierte Inhalte angelegt – z. B. Produktbeschreibung, Feature-Liste, FAQ, Release Notes, UI-Strings.
Strikte Trennung von Inhalt und Präsentation

: Kein Rich-Text-Chaos mit eingebetteten Überschriften, Links und Inline-Styles. Stattdessen klar definierte Felder und Komponenten. Portable Text hilft, flexible Inhalte trotzdem strukturiert zu halten.
Automatisierte Übergabe an Übersetzung
: Über Integrationen oder Exporte werden nur die relevanten Felder an Ihr TMS oder Ihre Agentur übergeben. Jeder Textbaustein hat eine eindeutige ID, sodass Änderungen nachvollziehbar bleiben.
Rückimport und QA im Kontext
: Übersetzte Inhalte landen wieder im Content Lake und können im Studio im Kontext der Zielseite geprüft werden – idealerweise mit einer Preview, die zeigt, wie der Text im finalen Layout wirkt.
Mit GROQ können Sie z. B. alle noch nicht übersetzten Felder für einen bestimmten Markt abfragen und gezielt anstoßen. Das spart Tage an manueller Koordination.
Regulatorische Freigaben: Legal früh einbauen, nicht am Ende „drüber schauen lassen“

Regulatorik ist der stille Launch-Killer. In einem Markt brauchen Sie einen Disclaimer im Checkout, im anderen eine explizite Einwilligung, im dritten dürfen Sie bestimmte Leistungsversprechen gar nicht machen.
Statt Legal am Ende „mal drüber schauen zu lassen“, binden Sie die Teams in den
Content-Workflow
ein:
Regulatorische Anforderungen als strukturierte Felder
: Legen Sie Felder wie
legalDisclaimer
regulatoryCategory
ageRestriction
an, die pro Markt befüllt werden können.
Perspectives für Marktansichten

: Mit Perspectives können Sie im Studio gezielt die Sicht eines bestimmten Marktes einnehmen – inklusive aller rechtlichen Texte. So sieht Legal exakt, was in „DE-Finanzprodukte“ oder „US-Healthcare“ live gehen wird.
Releases für abgestimmte Freigaben

: Nutzen Sie Releases, um alle Inhalte eines Launches zu bündeln. Legal gibt nicht einzelne Seiten frei, sondern ein gesamtes Release pro Markt. Änderungen nach Freigabe werden sichtbar und nachvollziehbar.
Ein praktischer Trick: Definieren Sie pro Markt einen
„Regulatory Risk Level“

(z. B. niedrig/mittel/hoch) und verknüpfen Sie diesen mit Pflichtfeldern im Content-Modell. In Hochrisiko-Märkten kann ein Launch-Release ohne ausgefüllte Legal-Felder gar nicht erst auf „bereit zum Go-live“ gesetzt werden.
Asset-Lokalisierung: Screenshots, Videos und UI-Strings im Griff behalten
Text ist nur die halbe Miete. Für einen glaubwürdigen Launch brauchen Sie
lokalisierte Screenshots, Videos und UI-Strings
, die zum tatsächlichen Produktstand im jeweiligen Markt passen.
Typische Stolperfallen:
Screenshots zeigen Features, die in bestimmten Märkten (noch) nicht verfügbar sind.
Video-Overlays enthalten englische UI-Labels, während das Produkt lokalisiert ist.
App-Store-Screenshots sind nicht mit der Live-Version synchron.
So vermeiden Sie das:
UI-Strings als Teil des Content-Modells
: Statt UI-Texte in Code zu verstecken, modellieren Sie sie im Content Lake – mit klaren Keys, Sprachen und Kontext. Das erleichtert sowohl Lokalisierung als auch QA.
Screenshot-Sets pro Markt
: Legen Sie im Studio eine Struktur an wie
screenshotSetGlobal
plus
screenshotSetByMarket
. Jedes Set ist mit einer Produktversion verknüpft, sodass klar ist, welche Screenshots zu welchem Feature-Stand gehören.
Video-Varianten planen
: Arbeiten Sie mit editierbaren Layern (z. B. für Text-Overlays), damit Sie nicht 12 komplett neue Videos produzieren müssen, sondern gezielt Text und UI-Ausschnitte austauschen können.
Ein bewährter Prozess: Zuerst wird die
globale Storyline
definiert, dann werden die
lokalen Abweichungen

als strukturierte Parameter modelliert (andere Währung, andere Feature-Verfügbarkeit, andere rechtliche Hinweise). Das Produktionsteam arbeitet dann mit diesen Parametern statt mit losem Feedback aus E-Mails.
Strukturierter Content als Rückgrat des Launches

Ohne strukturierten Content wird ein simultaner Launch schnell zu einem unkontrollierbaren Copy-Paste-Projekt. Mit einem sauber modellierten Content Lake dagegen können Sie Änderungen zentral steuern und sicherstellen, dass alle Kanäle konsistent sind.
Ein praxiserprobtes Modell umfasst u. a.:
Produkt-Entitäten
